region_of_origin: Southern China / Hong Kong / Southeast Asia

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Liang Teh is approximately 250 ml (one cup), as commonly sold at herbal tea stalls and Chinese medical halls in Singapore and Southeast Asia.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, herb, brewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 14355); supplemented with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Foods for herbal beverage category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Liang Teh is a broad category of Chinese herbal decoctions with no single USDA entry. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for brewed herbal tea (NDB 14355) and cross-referenced with Singapore HPB nutrient data for herbal beverages. Lightly sweetened commercial variants (e.g., Wang Lao Ji) may have higher sugar and energy values. The values here represent a lightly sweetened generic Liang Teh as commonly consumed. Fat_g is naturally zero for this aqueous herbal decoction. Saturated_fat_g is naturally zero by the same rationale.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate for lightly sweetened herbal tea decoction
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (University of Sydney GI Database collaborative publication)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Liang Teh / Chinese herbal tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened herbal tea and dilute sugar-water beverages with low carbohydrate concentration (3.8 g/100 ml). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 9.5) / 100 = 2.4. Unsweetened variants would have a GI approaching zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 ml) and moderate potassium (45 mg/100 ml) content
hypertension.guidance: Liang Teh is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content; however, patients on antihypertensive medications should consult a TCM practitioner before consuming strongly medicinal bitter varieties, as certain herbs may have mild diuretic or vasodilatory interactions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~25) and low carbohydrate load (3.8 g/100 ml); unsweetened variants have negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should choose the unsweetened (kosong) version of Liang Teh to avoid added sugar; the unsweetened form has a negligible glycemic load and may be consumed freely as part of a balanced diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, and presence of antioxidant phytochemicals from medicinal herbs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Liang Teh contains no fat or cholesterol and is rich in plant-derived antioxidants and polyphenols that may support vascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease can include unsweetened Liang Teh as a heart-friendly beverage choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from diverse medicinal herbs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The broad spectrum of anti-inflammatory phytochemicals in Liang Teh may provide mild complementary benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though it should not replace prescribed anti-inflammatory therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal Tea (涼茶, Liang Teh), literally meaning 'Cooling Tea', is a cornerstone of traditional Chinese preventive medicine and a widely consumed daily wellness beverage across Southern China, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia, including Singapore. It is not a single tea but a broad category of decoctions brewed from combinations of medicinal herbs, roots, flowers, and plant materials such as chrysanthemum, self-heal (Prunella vulgaris), honeysuckle, licorice root, and various bitter herbs. Preparation involves simmering or prolonged decoction of these botanicals in water, producing a beverage that ranges from dark, intensely bitter brews (such as '24 Herbs' or 'Wang Lao Ji') to lighter, mildly sweet versions. In TCM philosophy, Liang Teh is consumed to balance 'internal heat' (Shang Huo) — a condition believed to arise from consuming spicy or fried foods, staying up late, or exposure to hot weather. Nutritionally, Liang Teh is very low in calories (approximately 15 kcal per 100 ml), contains negligible fat and protein, and has a low carbohydrate content (3.8 g/100 ml), primarily from any added sugar. It has a low glycemic index (~25) and a glycemic load of approximately 2.4 per 250 ml serving. The beverage is naturally low in sodium (2 mg/100 ml) and provides modest amounts of potassium, magnesium, and trace minerals from the herbs. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (especially the unsweetened version), and cardiovascular disease. The unsweetened (kosong) version is recommended for those monitoring blood sugar or caloric intake. Liang Teh is vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-friendly.
common_user_queries: Is Liang Teh good for diabetes?
How many calories in herbal tea?
What is Liang Teh made of?
Is Wang Lao Ji the same as Liang Teh?
Can I drink herbal tea if I have high blood pressure?
Is Chinese herbal tea low GI?
What is the sugar content of Liang Teh?
What does Liang Teh taste like?
meal_context: after a heavy or heaty meal

confidence_reason: Liang Teh is a broad category of herbal decoctions with no single standardized nutrient composition. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brewed herbal tea entry and cross-referenced with Singapore HPB herbal beverage data. The GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium because the exact nutrient profile varies significantly by herb combination, preparation method, and degree of sweetening.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero for this aqueous herbal decoction; no lipid-contributing ingredients are present. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero or naturally zero for an unsweetened/lightly sweetened herbal tea decoction. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are reported as 0.0 as herbal teas contain negligible amounts of these nutrients per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355). Water_g estimated at 95.8 g/100 g based on the aqueous nature of the beverage (energy balance cross-check: 0.1g protein × 4 + 3.8g carb × 4 + 0g fat × 9 = 0.4 + 15.2 = 15.6 kcal ≈ 15 kcal reported, consistent). Glycemic load per serving (250 g): (25 × 9.5) / 100 = 2.4. The duplicate portion entry in the input JSON has been deduplicated to a single 250 ml serving. Ontology codes are defensible best-match entries; FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code should be verified against current ontology releases. Liang Teh sold commercially (e.g., canned Wang Lao Ji) may have significantly higher sugar content (up to 10–13 g/100 ml) and should be treated as a separate record.

improvement_actions_taken: Restructured input JSON into RESULT template format.
Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual local names: Malay (Teh Herba), Tamil (மூலிகை தேநீர்), Hindi (हर्बல चाय).
Renamed food_group from 'Side dishes' to 'Beverages' to correctly classify Liang Teh.
Added saturated_fat_g (0.0) and all other required per_100g fields missing from input.
Added water_g estimate (95.8) based on aqueous composition.
Justified fat_g = 0.0 in notes as naturally zero for aqueous herbal decoction.

Replaced input GI source (Journal of Food and Drug Analysis, not an approved GI source) with analogous estimate from American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database collaborative publication.
Added health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (machine key type2_diabetes), and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
Added ontology section with FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT defensible codes.
Expanded aliases_by_language with all mandatory language groups.
Verified aliases consistency: all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
Deduplicated duplicate portion entry from input.
Added LLM training section with 200+ character description, common queries, meal context, and ingredient synonyms.
Documented all zero-value macros and vitamins in notes per g4 and g3 rules.
